map.condition: Need to negate an implication or validate contrapositive
map.use:
p 	o q \equiv 
eg p \lor q \equiv 
eg q 	o 
eg p

map.condition: Checking if a WFF is a tautology / valid
map.use: \text{Valid iff true under ALL interpretations (truth table exhaustive)}

map.condition: Applying modus ponens or modus tollens in an argument
map.use: \frac{p,\; p \to q}{q} \quad \text{(MP)} \qquad \frac{\neg q,\; p \to q}{\neg p} \quad \text{(MT)}

map.condition: Converting to CNF or DNF for SAT / resolution
map.use: \text{CNF: product of sum-clauses}\quad \text{DNF: sum of product-terms}

map.condition: Simplifying using De Morgan or absorption
map.use:
eg(p \land q) \equiv 
eg p \lor 
eg q \qquad p \lor (p \land q) \equiv p

formulas.name: Implication (→) definition
formulas.latex: p \to q \equiv \neg p \lor q
formulas.whenToUse: Eliminate or expand an implication into disjunction for simplification
formulas.trap: p → q is FALSE only when p is TRUE and q is FALSE; many students confuse the F→T case (it is TRUE).

formulas.name: Biconditional (↔) definition
formulas.latex: p \leftrightarrow q \equiv (p \to q) \land (q \to p) \equiv (p \land q) \lor (\neg p \land \neg q)
formulas.whenToUse: Evaluate 'if and only if' statements; true exactly when both sides share the same truth value

terms.means: p and q have identical truth values

formulas.trap: Biconditional is NOT the same as exclusive-OR; p⊕q is TRUE when they differ, p↔q is TRUE when they are equal.

formulas.name: Exclusive OR (⊕) definition
formulas.latex: p \oplus q \equiv (p \lor q) \land \neg(p \land q) \equiv \neg(p \leftrightarrow q)
formulas.whenToUse: When the question distinguishes 'one or the other but not both'

terms.means: exactly one of p, q is true

formulas.trap: XOR is the negation of the biconditional, not of OR.

formulas.name: Contrapositive equivalence
formulas.latex: p \to q \equiv \neg q \to \neg p
formulas.whenToUse: Restate an implication via its contrapositive when one direction is easier to prove or derive
formulas.trap: Converse (q→p) and inverse (¬p→¬q) are NOT equivalent to the original implication; only the contrapositive is.

formulas.name: De Morgan's Laws
formulas.latex:
eg(p \land q) \equiv 
eg p \lor 
eg q \qquad 
eg(p \lor q) \equiv 
eg p \land 
eg q
formulas.whenToUse: Push negation inside a conjunction or disjunction; essential for CNF/DNF conversion
formulas.trap: Students frequently flip the connective the wrong way: ¬(p∧q) gives ∨, not ∧.

formulas.name: Double Negation
formulas.latex: \neg\neg p \equiv p
formulas.whenToUse: Simplify when a variable appears doubly negated
formulas.trap: Do not leave double negations unreduced when simplifying; they hide the literal.

formulas.name: Distributive Laws
formulas.latex: p \land (q \lor r) \equiv (p \land q) \lor (p \land r) \qquad p \lor (q \land r) \equiv (p \lor q) \land (p \lor r)
formulas.whenToUse: Expand or factor expressions during CNF/DNF conversion
formulas.trap: Distribution of ∨ over ∧ is often forgotten; both directions are valid.

formulas.name: Absorption Laws
formulas.latex: p \lor (p \land q) \equiv p \qquad p \land (p \lor q) \equiv p
formulas.whenToUse: Simplify redundant sub-expressions in a formula
formulas.trap: Students often skip absorption and leave unsimplified forms, leading to unnecessary CNF clauses.

formulas.name: Idempotent & Complement Laws
formulas.latex: p \lor p \equiv p \quad p \land p \equiv p \qquad p \lor \neg p \equiv T \quad p \land \neg p \equiv F
formulas.plain: p∨p ≡ p    p∧p ≡ p    p∨¬p ≡ T    p∧¬p ≡ F
formulas.whenToUse: Detect tautologies (p∨¬p) or contradictions (p∧¬p) quickly
formulas.trap: p∨¬p is a tautology regardless of p's value; do not evaluate it as undetermined.

formulas.name: Modus Ponens (MP)
formulas.latex: \frac{p \quad p \to q}{\therefore\, q}
formulas.whenToUse: Direct reasoning: given an implication and its antecedent, conclude the consequent
formulas.trap: Affirming the consequent (q, p→q ⊢ p) is INVALID and a common logical fallacy.

formulas.name: Modus Tollens (MT)
formulas.latex: \frac{\neg q \quad p \to q}{\therefore\, \neg p}
formulas.whenToUse: Indirect reasoning: the conclusion failed, so the antecedent must be false
formulas.trap: Denying the antecedent (¬p, p→q ⊢ ¬q) is INVALID; only MT (denying the consequent) is valid.

formulas.name: Hypothetical Syllogism (HS)
formulas.latex: \frac{p \to q \quad q \to r}{\therefore\, p \to r}
formulas.whenToUse: Chain two implications to obtain a transitive implication
formulas.trap: HS only works when the consequent of the first matches the antecedent of the second exactly.

formulas.name: Disjunctive Syllogism (DS)
formulas.latex: \frac{p \lor q \quad \neg p}{\therefore\, q}
formulas.whenToUse: Eliminate one disjunct when the other is known false
formulas.trap: DS requires one disjunct to be strictly false (¬p); a merely 'unknown' p does not suffice.

formulas.name: Resolution Principle
formulas.latex: \frac{(p \lor q) \quad (\neg p \lor r)}{\therefore\, q \lor r}
formulas.whenToUse: Automated theorem proving and SAT solving with CNF clauses
formulas.trap: Resolution requires the clauses to be in CNF; applying it to arbitrary formulas without conversion leads to errors.

formulas.name: Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)
formulas.latex: \text{CNF} = C_1 \land C_2 \land \cdots \land C_k \quad \text{where each } C_i = (l_{i1} \lor l_{i2} \lor \cdots)
formulas.plain: CNF = (clause1) ∧ (clause2) ∧ ... where each clause is a disjunction of literals
formulas.whenToUse: Required for resolution proofs and SAT solvers; any propositional formula can be converted to CNF
formulas.trap: A formula is satisfiable iff its CNF is; do not confuse a single unsatisfiable clause (contradiction) with the whole CNF being unsatisfiable.

formulas.name: Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF)
formulas.latex: \text{DNF} = M_1 \lor M_2 \lor \cdots \lor M_k \quad \text{where each } M_i = (l_{i1} \land l_{i2} \land \cdots)
formulas.plain: DNF = (minterm1) ∨ (minterm2) ∨ ... where each minterm is a conjunction of literals
formulas.whenToUse: Reading truth-table rows where the formula is TRUE; express as sum-of-products
formulas.trap: DNF may be exponentially large; for GATE questions check if a simpler equivalent exists.

formulas.name: CNF Conversion Algorithm
formulas.latex: 1.\;\text{Eliminate } \leftrightarrow,\to \quad 2.\;\text{Push } \neg \text{ inward (De Morgan)} \quad 3.\;\text{Distribute } \lor \text{ over } \land
formulas.plain: Step 1: Eliminate <-> and ->   Step 2: Push negations inward   Step 3: Distribute OR over AND
formulas.whenToUse: Converting any propositional formula to CNF before applying resolution
formulas.trap: Forgetting Step 1 (eliminating biconditionals and implications) before applying De Morgan leads to incorrect CNF.

formulas.name: Tautology (Valid WFF)
formulas.latex: \phi \text{ is a tautology} \iff \forall \text{interpretations } I:\; I(\phi) = T
formulas.plain: φ is valid iff φ is TRUE under every possible truth assignment
formulas.whenToUse: Check validity of a WFF; a formula is valid iff its negation is unsatisfiable

terms.means: an interpretation (truth assignment to all variables)

formulas.trap: Do not confuse 'valid' (always true) with 'satisfiable' (true under at least one interpretation).

formulas.name: Satisfiability & Contradiction
formulas.latex: \text{SAT}: \exists I\; I(\phi)=T \qquad \text{UNSAT (contradiction)}: \forall I\; I(\phi)=F
formulas.plain: Satisfiable: at least one truth assignment makes φ TRUE.   Contradiction: no assignment makes φ TRUE.
formulas.whenToUse: Classify a WFF; determine whether premises can simultaneously be true
formulas.trap: A tautology is always satisfiable; a contradiction is never satisfiable — these are not the same category.

formulas.name: Logical Consequence (Entailment)
formulas.latex: \{P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n\} \models Q \iff (P_1 \land P_2 \land \cdots \land P_n) \to Q \text{ is a tautology}
formulas.plain: {P1,...,Pn} |= Q  iff  (P1 ∧ ... ∧ Pn) -> Q  is a tautology
formulas.whenToUse: Verify that a conclusion Q follows necessarily from a set of premises; used in GATE deduction problems
formulas.trap: Entailment means the implication is a TAUTOLOGY, not just that the implication is true under some assignment.

formulas.name: Validity ↔ Negation Unsatisfiable
formulas.latex: \phi \text{ valid} \iff \neg\phi \text{ unsatisfiable}
formulas.plain: φ is valid  iff  ¬φ is unsatisfiable (contradiction)
formulas.whenToUse: Prove validity by refutation: negate the formula and show it leads to contradiction (used in resolution proofs)

terms.means: negation of the formula under test

formulas.trap: This duality is the foundation of proof by refutation; students forget to negate the target before starting resolution.

formulas.name: Converse, Inverse & Contrapositive
formulas.latex:
egin{aligned} 	ext{Original:} &\quad p 	o q \ 	ext{Contrapositive:} &\quad 
eg q 	o 
eg p \;(\equiv 	ext{original}) \ 	ext{Converse:} &\quad q 	o p \;(
ot\equiv 	ext{original}) \ 	ext{Inverse:} &\quad 
eg p 	o 
eg q \;(
ot\equiv 	ext{original}) \end{aligned}
formulas.plain: Original: p->q    Contrapositive: ¬q->¬p (equivalent)    Converse: q->p (not equiv)    Inverse: ¬p->¬q (not equiv)
formulas.whenToUse: GATE questions on which variants of an implication are equivalent to the original
formulas.trap: Converse and Inverse are equivalent to EACH OTHER (not to the original) — a frequent distractor in GATE options.

formulas.name: Chain Reasoning from Multiple Premises
formulas.latex: \text{If } P_1 \to P_2,\; P_2 \to P_3,\; \ldots,\; P_{k-1} \to P_k \text{ and } P_1, \text{ then } P_k
formulas.plain: If P1->P2->...->Pk and P1 is true, then Pk is true (transitive chain)
formulas.whenToUse: Logical reasoning problems with a chain of conditional statements (e.g. corrupt→not elected, kind→elected)
formulas.trap: Make sure every link in the chain is actually given as a premise before applying transitive closure.

formulas.name: Knights & Knaves (Truth-Teller/Liar) Reasoning
formulas.latex: \text{If person is Type 1 (truth-teller): statement} \equiv T.\quad \text{If Type 2 (liar): statement} \equiv F.
formulas.plain: Type 1 always truth: their claim is TRUE.   Type 2 always lies: their claim is FALSE.   Case-split on type.
formulas.whenToUse: Puzzles where people always tell the truth or always lie; use case analysis on the two types
formulas.trap: When a liar is asked about their own type, they still lie — both types claim to be truth-tellers, so the self-report gives no information.
